# Chapter 3.8 - Artifacts

## 3.8.0 Overview

Artifacts are the persistent project objects that carry A.A.S. design evidence, generated payloads, file outputs, validation results, presentation material, and runtime handoff records across Chat, Draw, Model, and Architect.

### 3.8.1 What an Artifact Is

**Artifact Definition:** An artifact is a persisted project object with payload, metadata, lineage, validation state, and workspace projections. It may be generated by an agent, uploaded by a user, produced by Hermes, derived from another artifact, or exported by A.A.S. \
**Project Object:** An artifact becomes part of A.A.S. only after the backend registers it as an artifact record. A file written by Hermes, a temporary scratch output, or a token stream is not an A.A.S. artifact until registration occurs. \
**Payload Plus Meaning:** An artifact is not only a file. It is a file or structured payload plus title, kind, primitive type, source move, source task, source records, related nodes, related branches, related tensions, related commits, validation state, version history, preview state, and event history. \
**Traceable Evidence:** Artifacts exist so design work can be inspected, reused, validated, compared, committed, exported, and explained. Every important artifact should answer where it came from, what it supports, what it affects, whether it is trusted, and what downstream work depends on it. \
**Workspace-Agnostic Truth:** An artifact is one canonical backend object that can appear differently in each workspace. Chat may show it as a preview card, Draw may place it on a board, Model may load it as geometry or analysis, and Architect may show it as an evidence satellite or linked child record.

### 3.8.2 What Is Not an Artifact

**Chat Message:** A chat message can reference, preview, request, or explain an artifact, but the message itself is not an artifact unless explicitly saved as a document artifact. \
**Raw Token Output:** Streaming assistant text is transient conversation output. It becomes an artifact only when saved into a registered document, report, prompt package, evaluation, or other artifact record. \
**Move:** A move is an operation or selected execution intent applied to the design world. It can generate artifacts, but it is not an artifact. \
**Affordance:** An affordance is a meaningful possible action exposed by the Field Runtime. It is not an artifact. \
**Direction Node:** Object, Subject, Vector, Boundary, and Seed nodes are semantic design matter. They are graph truth records, not artifact payloads. \
**Direction Link:** A link is a relationship between nodes or records. It is not an artifact. \
**Runtime Event:** An event records what happened. It is audit and replay infrastructure, not an artifact. A summarized execution trace can be registered as a Runtime Artifact when it becomes useful as an inspectable project object. \
**Hermes Local File:** A file written inside a Hermes task root is a candidate output. It becomes an A.A.S. artifact only when the A.A.S.-Hermes Bridge registers it with metadata, lineage, validation state, and event history. \
**Temporary Script:** A helper script used only inside one task and discarded is execution detail, not an artifact. If the same script is preserved for user interaction, reuse, validation, or audit, it can become an Interactive Artifact or Runtime Artifact.

### 3.8.3 Why Artifacts Exist

**Design Evidence:** Artifacts preserve evidence that supports or challenges design truth, such as site reports, code excerpts, climate data, model checks, critique reports, and validation outputs. \
**Design Production:** Artifacts carry generated architectural material, including concepts, models, plans, sections, diagrams, renders, boards, prompts, and export packages. \
**Cross-Workspace Continuity:** Artifacts allow Chat, Draw, Model, and Architect to operate on the same project material instead of creating separate workspace-local objects. \
**Lineage and Trust:** Artifacts make it possible to trace design work from goal, node, branch, tension, move, Hermes task, generated file, evaluator result, and commit. \
**Governed Commitment:** Artifacts may inform project truth, but they do not become project truth by existing. They become evidence for commits, validations, branch decisions, and finalization gates. \
**Agent Collaboration:** Agents use artifacts as durable shared context. Rather than relying on chat history or profile memory alone, they can inspect registered artifacts with explicit source and status. \
**User Validation:** The user can visually confirm what exists, what is missing, what is speculative, what is validated, and what has been accepted or committed.

### 3.8.4 Artifact Ontology Within A.A.S.

**Primary Ontology Boundary:** A.A.S. has five primary direction node types: Object, Subject, Vector, Boundary, and Seed. Artifact primitives do not add new direction node types. \
**Node as Meaning:** A direction node says what a piece of design matter means inside the project field. \
**Artifact as Payload:** An artifact shows what exists as evidence, generated material, analysis, representation, model data, interactive tool, runtime packet, or presentation output. \
**Link as Relationship:** Artifact links explain how an artifact matters: evidence, reference, input, output, validation, lineage, branch output, tension evidence, commit evidence, or derived material. \
**Validation as Trust State:** An artifact can be untested, passed, warning, failed, accepted, rejected, superseded, archived, or committed as evidence. \
**Commit as Truth Adoption:** An artifact can support a commit, but the artifact itself is not the commit. The commit records the decision, rationale, evidence, consequences, reversibility, and approval metadata. \
**Short Grammar:** Node says what it means. Artifact shows what exists. Link says how it matters. Validation says whether it can be trusted. Commit says whether it becomes project truth.

### 3.8.5 What an Artifact Primitive Is

**Primitive Definition:** An artifact primitive is a stable system-level archetype that describes the role an artifact plays in A.A.S. It is not a file extension, one-off file name, agent role, workspace view, phase label, or visual format. \
**Primitive Purpose:** Artifact primitives let the backend, frontend, evaluators, workspaces, bridge, artifact browser, and agents understand how an artifact should be stored, previewed, validated, linked, and reused. \
**Primitive Stability:** Artifact primitives are hard architecture. They should change slowly and rarely. They are closer to Object, Subject, Vector, Boundary, and Seed than to mutable project tags. \
**Kind Flexibility:** Artifact kind is the flexible vocabulary below primitive. For example, `visual` can contain `hero_render`, `atmosphere_study`, `material_reference`, or `segmentation_mask`. \
**Extension Boundary:** File extensions describe technical handling, not semantic primitive type. A `.html` file may be a Context Artifact if it is a saved web source, an Interactive Artifact if it is a user-facing tool, or a Presentation Artifact if it is a web export. \
**Primitive Rule:** Primitive equals stable system role. Kind equals flexible project vocabulary. Extension equals file handling detail.

### 3.8.6 Starting Artifact Primitive Types

| No. | Primitive Type       | Definition                                                                                                                                                             | Common File Extensions                                                           | Example                                                                                                                 |
| --: | -------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
|   1 | Context Artifact     | External or internal source material that informs Object, Subject, Vector, Boundary, or Seed reasoning.                                                                | `.md`, `.pdf`, `.html`, `.json`, `.csv`, `.jpg`, `.png`, `.webp`                 | Site report, climate table, code excerpt, user interview transcript, precedent pack.                                    |
|   2 | Value Artifact       | A structured artifact that expresses design values, priorities, direction state, Agent Direction State, WorldState summaries, or field-level project compass material. | `.json`, `.yaml`, `.md`, `.svg`, `.pdf`                                          | Agent Direction State, priority stack, value map, direction graph export, field snapshot summary.                       |
|   3 | Concept Artifact     | A design hypothesis artifact that develops a Seed, branch, scheme, spatial strategy, tension response, or possible architectural direction.                            | `.md`, `.json`, `.pdf`, `.svg`, `.png`                                           | Concept package, scheme option, branch proposal, massing idea sheet, tension-resolution option.                         |
|   4 | Geometry Artifact    | Spatial model payload or geometry source that supports ground-truth modeling, spatial validation, downstream drawings, and model-derived analysis.                     | `.3dm`, `.glb`, `.gltf`, `.obj`, `.stl`, `.ifc`, `.json`                         | Rhino ground-truth model, massing model, model metadata, IFC coordination model.                                        |
|   5 | Drawing Artifact     | Architectural drawing output derived from concept, model, analysis, or representation work.                                                                            | `.pdf`, `.svg`, `.png`, `.dwg`, `.dxf`, `.ai`                                    | Floor plan, section, elevation, axon, detail drawing, section cut sheet.                                                |
|   6 | Diagram Artifact     | Analytical or explanatory visual artifact that clarifies relationships, performance, flows, checks, or design logic.                                                   | `.svg`, `.png`, `.pdf`, `.json`, `.csv`                                          | Circulation diagram, solar analysis, adjacency graph, view analysis, area schedule, conflict map.                       |
|   7 | Visual Artifact      | Two-dimensional visual material that is not primarily a drawing or diagram.                                                                                            | `.png`, `.jpg`, `.jpeg`, `.webp`, `.tiff`, `.svg`, `.txt`, `.json`               | Hero render, atmosphere study, image prompt package, material swatch, mood image, segmentation mask.                    |
|   8 | Evaluation Artifact  | Critique, score, validation, QA, comparison, or evaluator output that measures artifact quality, branch quality, consistency, risk, or readiness.                      | `.json`, `.md`, `.csv`, `.pdf`, `.png`                                           | Critique report, feature score sheet, render/model consistency report, board QA report, branch comparison score.        |
|   9 | Runtime Artifact     | Execution-facing artifact used to inspect, replay, reproduce, or audit A.A.S.-Hermes work.                                                                             | `.json`, `.yaml`, `.log`, `.txt`, `.md`, `.patch`, `.diff`                       | Hermes task packet, source manifest, bridge event summary, artifact lineage manifest, agent patch proposal.             |
|  10 | Interactive Artifact | User-facing or agent-facing mini software object that supports collaboration, analysis, comparison, calculation, or interactive reporting.                             | `.html`, `.css`, `.js`, `.ts`, `.tsx`, `.jsx`, `.json`, `.wasm`, `.ipynb`, `.py` | Slope calculator, branch comparison UI, daylight analysis viewer, interactive QA report, embedded design decision tool. |

### 3.8.7 Primitive Classification Logic

**System Role Test:** Ask what A.A.S. does with the artifact. If the artifact primarily supplies context, expresses value direction, develops a concept, stores geometry, represents a drawing, explains analysis, carries visual imagery, evaluates quality, records runtime evidence, or provides an interactive tool, it maps to one of the ten primitives. \
**Lifecycle Test:** Ask how the artifact moves through candidate, provisional, active, accepted, committed, superseded, rejected, or archived states. Artifacts with similar lifecycle behavior should share primitives. \
**Evaluator Test:** Ask which evaluator or validator reads the artifact. Concept, geometry, drawing, diagram, visual, evaluation, presentation, runtime, and interactive artifacts require different validation expectations. \
**Workspace Test:** Ask where the user inspects or manipulates the artifact. Chat previews, Draw placements, Model layers, Architect satellites, and sidebar viewers are workspace projections of the same artifact record. \
**Lineage Test:** Ask what the artifact must link to. A strong primitive classification should clarify whether the artifact links to Object, Subject, Vector, Boundary, Seed, branch, tension, commit, move, Hermes task, board, model binding, or another artifact. \
**Consolidation Rule:** If a new file differs only by extension, format, label, agent source, workspace view, or project phase, it should become a new artifact kind or handler under an existing primitive, not a new primitive.

### 3.8.8 Encountering New Artifact-Like Data

**Step One — Persistence Check:** If the data is transient, scratch-only, token-only, or task-local with no downstream value, it is not registered as an artifact. \
**Step Two — Registration Check:** If the data must persist beyond the task, be inspected by the user, be reused by agents, support a decision, or appear in a workspace, the backend creates or updates an artifact record. \
**Step Three — Primitive Assignment:** The backend or bridge assigns the closest existing primitive based on system role, lifecycle, evaluator, workspace, and lineage tests. \
**Step Four — Kind Assignment:** The backend assigns a specific artifact kind such as `slope_calculator`, `hero_render`, `section_cut`, `climate_report`, `branch_score`, `world_state_snapshot`, or `task_packet`. \
**Step Five — Handler Selection:** The file handler, viewer, previewer, sandbox, parser, or model loader is selected from file metadata and primitive requirements. \
**Step Six — Link Creation:** The artifact is linked to source move, source task, source artifact, relevant nodes, branches, tensions, commits, evaluations, boards, or model bindings. \
**Step Seven — Validation:** Required validators run when the artifact affects model truth, drawing truth, board finalization, execution safety, code execution, or committed project evidence. \
**Step Eight — Event Emission:** Artifact creation, update, validation, failure, acceptance, supersession, archive, or commit support emits runtime events and refreshes WorldState.

### 3.8.9 Living Library of Artifact Primitives

**Library Shape:** A.A.S. maintains a small Artifact Primitive Registry, a larger Artifact Kind Registry, and a File Handler Registry. These are separate layers. \
**Artifact Primitive Registry:** Stores hard system archetypes such as Context, Value, Concept, Geometry, Drawing, Diagram, Visual, Evaluation, Runtime, and Interactive. \
**Artifact Kind Registry:** Stores flexible vocabulary under primitives, such as `site_report`, `taste_board`, `branch_package`, `massing_model`, `floor_plan`, `solar_analysis`, `hero_render`, `qa_report`, `task_packet`, or `branch_comparison_ui`. \
**File Handler Registry:** Stores extension and MIME behavior, such as image preview, PDF viewer, markdown renderer, model viewer, SVG viewer, sandboxed webview, JSON inspector, CSV table, or ZIP package browser. \
**No Bloat Rule:** New extension does not create a new primitive. New visual form does not create a new primitive. New project phase does not create a new primitive. New agent role does not create a new primitive. New workspace projection does not create a new primitive. \
**Proposal Path:** Agents may propose a new primitive only when existing primitives cannot represent the system role and the candidate requires different lifecycle, validation, viewer, permissions, evaluator behavior, or governance. \
**Lifecycle:** Proposed primitives pass through proposed, sandbox, active, monitored, deprecated, merged alias, archived, or rejected states. \
**Curator Governance:** Primitive promotion requires sandbox results, critic review, evaluator coverage, supervisor approval, documentation update, schema review, UI review, migration plan, compatibility handling, and tests. \
**Maintenance:** Periodic maintenance checks primitive and kind usage, duplicate kinds, stale kinds, unsafe kinds, confusing vocabulary, failed handlers, missing validators, and overlap with existing primitives. Maintenance may merge, alias, archive, deprecate, or quarantine kinds. It should almost never add primitives automatically. \
**Hermes Analogy:** Hermes skills can be proposed, refined, pruned, archived, or promoted after review. Artifact primitives should follow the same philosophy: agents can discover and propose, but stable system capability changes only through governed review.

### 3.8.10 Relationship Between Artifact Primitives and Node Primitives

**Object Relationship:** Context, Diagram, Evaluation, Geometry, Drawing, and Interactive Artifacts may prove, measure, or validate objective outside truth. Example: a site survey PDF supports an Object node for existing grade. \
**Subject Relationship:** Context, Visual, Value, and Concept Artifacts may record or express subjective inside truth. Example: a mood board supports a Subject node for quiet amber atmosphere. \
**Vector Relationship:** Value, Concept, Diagram, Visual, and Evaluation Artifacts may express the pressure the design should move toward. Example: an atmosphere direction sheet supports a Vector node for low warm light. \
**Boundary Relationship:** Context, Diagram, Evaluation, Geometry, Drawing, and Interactive Artifacts may define, test, or validate limits. Example: a slope calculator supports a Boundary node for accessible ramp constraints. \
**Seed Relationship:** Concept, Geometry, Drawing, Diagram, Visual, Evaluation, Presentation, and Interactive Artifacts may develop, test, compare, or represent a generated design possibility. Example: a render, section, and branch comparison UI all attach to a Seed branch. \
**Nesting Rule:** A direction node may visually contain artifact children, but the artifact remains an artifact record. Parent-child display in Architect does not convert the artifact into a primary node. \
**Linking Rule:** Artifact-to-node relationships are explicit artifact links with relation type, strength, confidence, source, and event history. Proximity, satellites, badges, and child rows are UI projections of these relationships. \
**Semantic Edit Rule:** Editing what an idea means updates the direction node. Editing the payload updates the artifact. Editing where the artifact appears updates workspace placement. Committing a decision creates a commit linked to artifacts and nodes.

### 3.8.11 Artifact Manifestation in Chat Workspace

**Message Preview:** Chat can show artifact preview cards, attachment chips, generated file cards, image previews, model-ready notices, validation summaries, interactive-app launch chips, and export links. \
**Message Link:** A chat message may reference an artifact through `chat_message_artifacts`. The preview is not the artifact; it is a projection of the artifact record. \
**Agent Output:** When an agent generates a file, Chat should show the registered artifact only after backend registration or candidate status is visible. It should not pretend a local worker file is accepted project material before bridge ingestion. \
**Open in Sidebar:** Artifact cards can open the shared Artifact Rail to preview, inspect lineage, compare versions, validate, link to nodes, place in Draw, load in Model, or show in Architect. \
**Move Connection:** Chat can present next moves created by the artifact, such as revise render, validate model consistency, commit branch, compare alternatives, ask user, or export board. \
**Truth Boundary:** Chat prose is not project truth. Chat can create commands, approvals, artifact registrations, and links, but the backend remains authoritative.

### 3.8.12 Artifact Manifestation in Draw Workspace

**Asset Shelf:** Draw consumes the shared artifact registry as a board-authoring asset shelf. Visual, Drawing, Diagram, Context, Concept, Evaluation, and Presentation Artifacts can become board material. \
**Placement Not Duplication:** Placing an artifact on a board creates a board element or workspace placement. It does not duplicate the canonical artifact unless the user exports or derives a new artifact. \
**Visual Composition:** Renders, diagrams, plans, sections, material swatches, captions, prompt notes, references, and evaluation badges can be arranged while preserving lineage back to source nodes, branches, tensions, commits, moves, and Hermes tasks. \
**Document Formatting:** A document artifact can appear as a formatted text block, excerpt, citation plate, or appendix panel. The underlying artifact remains versioned and inspectable in the Artifact Rail. \
**Geometry Capture:** Geometry Artifacts can appear in Draw as captured views, thumbnails, axons, model screenshots, plan cuts, or section cuts. These captures may be derived Drawing, Diagram, or Visual Artifacts depending on their role. \
**Export Path:** A board export creates a Presentation Artifact. It should preserve links to placed artifacts and finalization validations.

### 3.8.13 Artifact Manifestation in Model Workspace

**Primary Model Payload:** Geometry Artifacts are loaded into Model as ground-truth, hypothesis, available, outdated, invalid, validated, or superseded model states. \
**Model Layers:** Model Mode can show geometry versions, layers, object selections, analysis overlays, section planes, camera states, and Rhino Compute results as artifact-bound state. \
**Derived Outputs:** Plan cuts, section cuts, elevation cuts, area schedules, view analyses, solar studies, privacy checks, and render-perspective validations are Drawing, Diagram, or Evaluation Artifacts derived from Geometry Artifacts. \
**User Visibility:** Model Mode lets the user see what the agent is using as spatial ground truth. It should show linked commits, branches, tensions, validation state, and outdated warnings. \
**Agent Computation:** Rhino Compute or model tools operate through typed moves. Model Mode displays the artifact and derived results; it does not let raw geometry operations mutate A.A.S. truth outside backend commands. \
**Consistency Checks:** Visual Artifacts such as renders can be compared against Geometry Artifacts. Failed checks create Evaluation Artifacts, warnings, tensions, or recovery moves.

### 3.8.14 Artifact Manifestation in Architect Workspace

**Semantic Field:** Architect Mode shows nodes as design meaning and artifacts as attached evidence, outputs, references, validations, or child payloads. \
**Artifact Satellites:** Artifacts can appear as thumbnail satellites, child chips, evidence markers, output markers, validation lamps, lineage lines, missing-artifact ghosts, or expandable child rows near their linked nodes. \
**Node Selection:** Selecting a node filters the Artifact Rail and field overlays to linked artifacts, missing required artifacts, failed validations, candidate outputs, and commit evidence. \
**Artifact Selection:** Selecting an artifact can highlight the nodes, branches, tensions, commits, moves, and Hermes tasks it connects to. \
**Output Map:** Output Map emphasizes artifacts, deliverables, model versions, renders, missing packets, board components, and finalization readiness. \
**Truth Filter:** Truth Filter emphasizes Context, Diagram, Evaluation, Geometry, and Interactive Artifacts that support Object and Boundary nodes. \
**Taste Isolation:** Taste Isolation emphasizes Context, Value, Concept, and Visual Artifacts that support Subject and Vector nodes. \
**Branch Compare:** Branch Compare groups Concept, Geometry, Drawing, Diagram, Visual, Evaluation, and Interactive Artifacts by Seed branch. \
**Anti-Confusion Rule:** Artifacts may look node-like when shown as satellites, but their visual treatment should remain secondary. They are payload objects attached to the semantic graph, not new primary node primitives.

### 3.8.15 Shared Sidebar Artifact Rail

**Purpose:** The Artifact Rail is the consistent source-of-truth artifact viewer across Chat, Draw, Model, and Architect. It is not a raw file browser. It is the project evidence, payload, validation, lineage, and usage console. \
**Persistent Placement:** The Artifact Rail should be available from the shared right sidebar or inspector area in every workspace. It should preserve the same vocabulary, status states, primitive categories, filters, and preview behavior across modes. \
**All Tab:** Shows the full project artifact registry grouped by primitive, kind, branch, phase, status, validation state, or recent activity. \
**Linked Tab:** Shows artifacts linked to the current selection, such as a chat message, node, branch, tension, board element, model object, commit, move, or Hermes task. \
**Needs Tab:** Shows missing required artifacts needed for validation, commit, board export, model checks, branch comparison, or finalization. \
**Validation Tab:** Shows untested, passed, warning, failed, stale, superseded, rejected, and accepted artifacts. \
**Lineage Tab:** Shows source move, Hermes task, source artifacts, derived artifacts, evaluator outputs, branch links, tension links, commit links, and export chain. \
**Preview Tab:** Shows the current artifact through the correct viewer, such as markdown, PDF, image, SVG, model viewer, table, JSON inspector, sandboxed webview, patch viewer, or ZIP/package browser. \
**Workspace Action Controls:** The rail can expose actions such as open in workspace, place in Draw, load in Model, show in Architect, attach to Chat, validate, compare, derive, supersede, archive, or propose commit evidence. \
**Visual Trust:** The rail should make artifact status visually obvious: candidate, provisional, active, accepted, committed, superseded, rejected, archived, untested, warning, failed, and passed should be readable without opening raw metadata.

### 3.8.16 Backend Artifact Data Model

**Canonical Artifact Record:** `artifacts` stores the primary artifact identity, project, primitive type, kind, title, description, lifecycle status, validation state, current version, source move, source task binding, metadata, creator, timestamps, and version. \
**Version Record:** `artifact_versions` stores artifact version number, associated file, preview, source version, change summary, creator, timestamp, and status. \
**File Record:** `artifact_files` stores storage URL or path, MIME type, extension, checksum, size, dimensions, duration, entrypoint path, and security metadata. \
**Link Record:** `artifact_links` stores artifact-to-record relationships, including target type, target ID, relation type, strength, confidence, source, creator, and version. \
**Preview Record:** `artifact_previews` stores thumbnails, text previews, extracted pages, model captures, SVG previews, web app previews, JSON summaries, and other derived viewing data. \
**Validation Record:** `artifact_validations` stores validator type, evaluator output, status, score JSON, warnings, evidence, confidence, source move, and created event. \
**Requirement Record:** `artifact_requirements` stores required artifact type, kind, reason, target record, required-for condition, status, waiver state, and satisfaction link. \
**Placement Record:** `artifact_placements` stores workspace-specific placement or view state for Chat, Draw, Model, and Architect without duplicating the artifact payload. \
**Board Records:** `boards` and `board_elements` store Draw board packages and artifact placements on board canvases. \
**Model Binding Records:** `model_artifact_bindings` store geometry artifact usage, model role, layer state, camera state, section plane, analysis state, and derived output links. \
**Chat Message Link Records:** `chat_message_artifacts` store which artifacts appear in which chat messages and how they are previewed. \
**Runtime Event Records:** `runtime_events` store artifact creation, registration, ingestion, validation, versioning, linking, placement, supersession, rejection, archive, and commit-support events.

### 3.8.17 Core Artifact Relationships

| Relationship                           | Storage Pattern                                                             | Example                                                  |
| -------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| Artifact proves a node                 | `artifact_links.target_type = direction_node`, `relation_type = evidence`   | Climate report supports Object node for desert heat.     |
| Artifact expresses a node              | `artifact_links.target_type = direction_node`, `relation_type = reference`  | Mood board supports Subject node for quiet warmth.       |
| Artifact constrains a node             | `artifact_links.target_type = direction_node`, `relation_type = validation` | Slope calculator validates Boundary node for ramp limit. |
| Artifact develops a Seed               | `artifact_links.target_type = direction_node`, `relation_type = output`     | Render belongs to Seed node for courtyard scheme.        |
| Artifact belongs to a branch           | `artifact_links.target_type = branch`, `relation_type = branch_output`      | Section cut belongs to Branch A.                         |
| Artifact documents a tension           | `artifact_links.target_type = tension`, `relation_type = tension_evidence`  | Comparison report explains privacy versus openness.      |
| Artifact supports a commit             | `artifact_links.target_type = commit`, `relation_type = commit_evidence`    | QA report supports section-first ground-truth commit.    |
| Artifact is generated by a move        | `artifacts.source_move_id` or `artifact_links.target_type = move`           | Hero render generated by atmosphere-study move.          |
| Artifact comes from Hermes             | `artifacts.source_task_binding_id`                                          | Task packet output becomes registered artifact.          |
| Artifact derives from another artifact | `artifact_links.target_type = artifact`, `relation_type = derived_from`     | Section SVG derives from Rhino model.                    |
| Artifact appears in Chat               | `chat_message_artifacts.artifact_id`                                        | Agent message shows render preview.                      |
| Artifact appears in Draw               | `board_elements.artifact_id`                                                | Board places plan and render.                            |
| Artifact appears in Model              | `model_artifact_bindings.artifact_id`                                       | GLB loaded in viewer.                                    |
| Artifact appears in Architect          | `artifact_links` plus optional `architect_artifact_proxies`                 | Render satellite under Seed node.                        |

### 3.8.18 Artifact Lifecycle and Trust States

**Candidate:** The artifact has been detected or proposed but is not yet accepted as useful project material. \
**Provisional:** The artifact is registered and visible but still needs review, validation, linking, or user confirmation. \
**Active:** The artifact is currently used by the project, workspace, branch, move, or field state. \
**Accepted:** The artifact has passed required review or validation for its local purpose. \
**Committed:** The artifact supports a committed design decision or finalization state. \
**Superseded:** A newer artifact version or replacement artifact has taken priority, but the older artifact remains inspectable. \
**Rejected:** The artifact was reviewed and rejected as misleading, low quality, invalid, or not useful. \
**Archived:** The artifact is preserved for history but hidden from normal active workspace views. \
**Untested:** No validator has evaluated the artifact. \
**Passed:** Required validation checks passed. \
**Warning:** Validation found issues that do not necessarily block continued work. \
**Failed:** Validation failed and should create warnings, tensions, recovery moves, or block finalization depending on severity.

### 3.8.19 Interactive Artifacts

**Definition:** An Interactive Artifact is a persisted mini software object that supports user collaboration, calculation, comparison, review, visualization, simulation, or interactive reporting. \
**Examples:** Slope calculator, ramp calculator, branch comparison UI, daylight viewer, option scoring explorer, code-matrix browser, interactive board QA report, model-analysis dashboard, or annotation tool. \
**Artifact Rule:** An interactive app is an artifact when it is registered, persisted, previewable, linked to project records, and useful beyond transient execution. \
**Not Artifact:** A one-off helper script used only inside a Hermes task is not an artifact unless registered for reuse, inspection, validation, or user interaction. \
**Sandbox Rule:** Interactive Artifacts require sandboxing, permission controls, input manifests, output contracts, validation, and clear separation from backend truth mutation. \
**Output Rule:** An Interactive Artifact may emit results, selections, annotations, calculations, or patch proposals, but it must not directly mutate A.A.S. product truth. All writes must pass through backend commands, approvals, version checks, and events. \
**Workspace Rule:** Chat can launch it, Architect can attach it to a node, Draw can capture or embed it, Model can use its outputs as analysis inputs, and the Artifact Rail can preview it in a sandboxed viewer.

### 3.8.20 Artifact Governance Rules

**Registration Rule:** No generated or uploaded file becomes an A.A.S. artifact until the backend registers it. \
**Lineage Rule:** Every important artifact should link to source move, source task, source records, source artifacts, branch, tension, commit, evaluation, or workspace placement where applicable. \
**Primitive Rule:** Use existing artifact primitives unless a new candidate passes governance gates. \
**Kind Rule:** Most new data types become artifact kinds, not primitives. \
**Handler Rule:** New file extensions create or reuse file handlers, not primitives. \
**Validation Rule:** Artifacts used for finalization, committed evidence, geometry truth, drawing truth, board export, interactive tools, or risky decisions require validation. \
**Workspace Rule:** Workspaces project artifacts; they do not own separate artifact truth. \
**Memory Boundary:** Hermes memory and profile recall may reference artifacts, but memory does not define artifact truth. \
**Bridge Boundary:** Hermes outputs become A.A.S. artifacts only through bridge-controlled registration and backend commands. \
**Commit Boundary:** Artifacts can support truth, but committed design truth changes only through commits.

### 3.8.21 Artifact Anti-Patterns

**File Browser as Truth:** Do not treat raw folders or local files as the artifact system. The artifact registry is the source of truth. \
**Primitive Bloat:** Do not create new artifact primitives for every new file type, report type, app type, agent output, workspace view, or project phase. \
**Artifact as Sixth Node:** Do not add Artifact as a primary direction node type. Artifacts link to Object, Subject, Vector, Boundary, and Seed nodes. \
**Workspace Duplication:** Do not create separate artifact copies for Chat, Draw, Model, and Architect when one canonical artifact with workspace placements is enough. \
**Unregistered Hermes Output:** Do not show a Hermes-written file as accepted project material before bridge registration. \
**Hidden Validation Failure:** Do not let failed, stale, or superseded artifacts remain visually indistinguishable from accepted artifacts. \
**Direct Interactive Mutation:** Do not let Interactive Artifacts mutate backend truth directly. They must emit structured outputs or patch proposals through commands. \
**Commit Confusion:** Do not treat an artifact as committed design truth just because it looks final. A final-looking render or board still requires commit and validation rules. \
**Lineage Loss:** Do not export or duplicate artifacts without preserving source links, version history, and downstream relationships.

### 3.8.22 Short Artifact Grammar

**Node:** What the design matter means. \
**Artifact:** What exists as payload, evidence, representation, model, tool, output, or trace. \
**Primitive:** Stable role the artifact plays in A.A.S. \
**Kind:** Specific vocabulary under the primitive. \
**File:** Technical payload handled by viewers, parsers, sandboxes, and storage. \
**Link:** Why the artifact matters to nodes, branches, tensions, commits, moves, and tasks. \
**Validation:** Whether the artifact can be trusted for its purpose. \
**Placement:** How the artifact appears inside a workspace. \
**Commit:** Whether the artifact supports project truth. \
**Artifact Rail:** Shared evidence console across all workspaces.
